_readme: Hand-annotated expense_management policies in the style of real corporate travel-and-expense rules; a held-out test set, never used for training.
- Actions: the tools an agent may call. Each policy guards when a tool call is allowed or denied.

- Ontology: the action-scoped canonical vocabulary for model outputs, grouped as expense_categories, numeric_fields, boolean_fields, enum_fields, and prerequisite_actions. Rule graphs must use only values grounded in this ontology. Runtime cases use ontology fields and may include non-canonical contrast values to test negative conditions.

- Compatibility: minimal generation/audit constraints describing low-controversy business-plausible combinations of categories, numeric fields, enum scopes, and prerequisites. These constraints are not model output; they are used to avoid schema-valid but business-implausible synthetic rules.
- Rule shape: each single rule clause is translated into one JSON rule object with a target_action and a deny_when list. Entries in deny_when are read as a conjunction (AND condition).
- Aggregation: multiple rule clauses from a policy document correspond to multiple JSON rule objects; these objects are aggregated in the rules list and read as a disjunction (OR condition).

- Decision: binary decision by design. A proposed call is DENY if any rule fires, ALLOW otherwise. This system has no separate REVIEW/ESCALATE outcome. Policy rules with review/escalate-routing language mean the agent may not execute the tool call automatically, therefore they are classified as DENY here for simplicity. Future work can extend this to more granular classification outcomes such as ALLOW/DENY/REVIEW.

- Safety: state describes the proposed call itself, while completed_actions records what the agent has already done. Field conditions fail open when the field is absent; prerequisite checks fail closed when the required completion is absent.
- Input: the model input is the policy text, wrapped with the ontology. Runtime cases are for evaluation only, not model input.
- Output: the model outputs only the JSON rule graph.
- Runtime cases: annotated boundary and contrast cases are created for policy-engine tests and evaluation metric calculation. They are not model input or model output.
- Evaluation: the policy engine evaluates a rule graph against a proposed tool call, given state and completed_actions from each runtime case.

- Audit: original policy text is kept alongside the model-output JSON graph for annotation and audit traceability, but it is not passed to the policy engine during evaluation. The policy engine reads the JSON rule graph, without the original policy text, to determine ALLOW or DENY.

actions.approve_expense_report: record approval of a submitted expense report; reversible, releases no money
actions.issue_reimbursement: pay an approved expense back to the employee; the money leaves and cannot be recalled
actions.increase_card_limit: raise the spending limit on a company card; enlarges what can be spent before any expense exists

policies.policy_text: An expense report may not be approved when it contains a single card charge above $3,000.
Alcoholic beverages are not eligible for reimbursement.
An expense report submitted more than 120 days after the expense date may not be approved automatically.
A rental car is reimbursable only when it was booked through an approved rental provider.
An expense report flagged as a possible duplicate submission may not be approved automatically.
A reimbursement for a flight booked fewer than seven days before departure may not be issued automatically.
A reimbursement may not be issued until the expense report has been approved.
An expense report containing a card charge of $1,000 or more may not be approved automatically.
An expense report containing a card charge from a merchant category that is not on the approved list may not be approved.
An expense report containing a card charge for a cash advance may not be approved.
An expense report containing a card charge may not be approved unless the cardholder has completed card training.
A reimbursement paid in a currency other than the employee's local currency may not be issued automatically.
A travel-card limit may not be raised above $3,000.
A requested card-limit increase of $1,000 or more may not be raised automatically.
A card limit may not be raised on a card that is not active.
A card limit may not be raised on a card flagged for suspected misuse.
A card limit may not be raised unless the cardholder has completed card training.
Fees for extra legroom or preferred seating are non-reimbursable.
All mini-bar purchases are strictly non-reimbursable.
Daily travel from home to the primary office is non-reimbursable.
Speeding tickets and parking fines are the employee's responsibility and are not reimbursable.
Meals for spouses or partners are not covered by the company.
Technical training bootcamps may not be approved on a corporate card.
Travel costs for family members accompanying employees are not reimbursable.
A reimbursement may not be issued for a flight booked fewer than 14 days before departure.
An expense report submitted more than 60 days after the expense date may not be approved.
First-class airfare is not reimbursable on any corporate route.
Basic Economy fares are not reimbursable due to their restrictive change and baggage terms.
Fees for in-flight movies, games, or headset rentals are not reimbursable.
In-flight alcoholic beverages are not reimbursable.
Fees for transporting personal pets or emotional-support animals are not reimbursable.
Airfare for a family member traveling with the employee is not reimbursable.
Personal accident insurance offered by rental companies is not reimbursable.
Expenditures at entertainment facilities, lounges, or venues are not reimbursable.
Casino chips, sports betting, racetrack wagers, and gambling losses are not reimbursable.
Private dining, country club, or social club membership fees are not reimbursable.
Residential heating, electricity, water, or gas expenses are not reimbursable.
Personal cloud storage expansions are not reimbursable.
Massages, facials, saunas, and luxury cosmetic spa treatments are not reimbursable.
Protein powders, vitamins, weight-loss plans, and dietary supplements are not reimbursable.
Purchasing gift cards or cash equivalents as client incentives is not reimbursable.
Individual travel meals are capped at a hard maximum of $75 per full day.
Ergonomic office chair purchases are reimbursable up to a maximum of $250.
A one-time home office equipment stipend is capped at $500.
A monthly home internet stipend is capped at $50.
Annual tuition reimbursement is capped at a hard maximum of $5,250.
An expense report may not be approved unless a business purpose is provided.
A reimbursement may not be issued unless the required tax identification number is provided.
Alcohol charges may not be reimbursed when the per-person alcohol limit is exceeded.
A temporary card limit may not be raised until the Controller has signed off.
Lounge day-passes are not covered unless explicitly pre-approved.
Early check-in or late check-out fees require business justification.
Private drivers require prior VP-level approval.
Tickets to industry conferences require VP sign-off before they can be approved.
ATM cash withdrawals on corporate cards are prohibited without prior approval.
Group meals must list all attendees before they can be approved.
Meal receipts must be itemized before reimbursement may be issued.
A reimbursement for a flight booked fewer than 14 days before departure may not be issued unless business justification has been documented.
Nightly hotel rates in North America are capped at $450 before taxes.
Business meals with clients are capped at $120 per person, including tax and tip.
Tangible corporate gifts for a client are capped at an annual limit of $100 per contact.
Sports or theater tickets for client entertainment are capped at $200 per ticket.
Internal staff dinners without clients present are limited to $50 per person.
City parking garage expenses for daytime business meetings are capped at $40 per day.
Restaurant tips are capped at 20% of the pre-tax bill in regions where tipping is standard.
Premium rideshare options such as Uber Black or Lyft Lux are not reimbursable unless the standard-ride wait is at least 30 minutes.
Hotel suite upgrades require manager approval before reimbursement may be issued.
Valet parking requires manager approval before reimbursement may be issued.
Extra legroom seats require written manager approval prior to booking.
A corporate airline lounge membership requires VP authorization.
A generative AI tool subscription may not be approved without CIO procurement clearance.
An encrypted external storage drive may not be approved without IT security approval.
Chauffeur services are not reimbursable unless pre-authorized by a C-suite executive.
Charitable gala tables may not be approved without written CEO authorization.
Every hospitality claim must document the business purpose before it can be approved.
Certification exam costs are not reimbursable until the exam has been passed.
A personal printer may not be approved without legal team approval.
A personal mileage claim exceeding 300 miles requires manager approval before reimbursement may be issued.
A client entertainment event over $1,500 requires VP approval before it can be approved.
A short-term rental of five or more nights requires written manager approval.
